blocks.text: Chapter 4.
Variable Reward: The Itch and the Beautiful Uncertainty
Now we reach the heart of the machine, friend — the chapter that explains the little thrill when you pull to refresh, and why you keep pulling even when nothing good has arrived in days.

The trigger fired. The action was made effortless. The user has tapped. And here the naive builder makes the fatal mistake: give them what they came for, every time, reliably. Sounds right. Kills the habit. Because the brain, it turns out, is not wired to crave reward.
It is wired to crave almost.
The pigeon that taught us everything
In the 1950s, the psychologist B.F. Skinner put pigeons in boxes with a lever that dispensed food. Peck the lever, get a pellet. The pigeons learned it quickly and pecked when hungry. Sensible birds.
Then Skinner changed one thing.
Pause and guess: He made the lever unpredictable — sometimes a pellet, sometimes nothing, on a random schedule. What do you think happened to the pecking?
Ready? Here is what happened.

It exploded. The pigeons pecked more, far more — compulsively, hungry or not — than they ever had when the reward was guaranteed. And the neuroscience that came later explains why: the dopamine system — the brain's engine of wanting — surges not when we get the reward but when we anticipate it, and uncertainty multiplies the surge. Predictable rewards get processed once and filed away; the brain stops responding to what it can foresee. But a maybe — a maybe never lets the brain file it. That is your pull-to-refresh. That is the slot machine's whole business model. That is why the feed shows you nothing twenty times and something wonderful the twenty-first — because a reward that comes every time would, strangely, bore you into leaving. The craving lives in the not-knowing.
Look once at a slot machine and once at your phone and see the same device: a lever, a spin of uncertainty, an occasional prize. One is regulated as gambling. The other is in your pocket.
The three hungers
But "reward" is not one thing. Eyal sorts the maybes into three ancient hungers — and every product that owns you is feeding at least one.

Rewards of the Tribe — the social maybes. Likes, replies, upvotes, followers: unpredictable morsels of other people's approval. We are herd animals; being valued by the tribe was once survival itself. So each notification is a tiny verdict on our standing — and because you never know when praise arrives or how warm it will be, you check. Stack Overflow's users write expert answers for free, for points — the points are a running scoreboard of tribal respect.

Rewards of the Hunt — the material maybes. Our ancestors chased unpredictable game across the savanna; we scroll unpredictable feeds for information, deals, news, gossip. The feed is a hunting ground engineered to be endless: the next flick of the thumb might reveal the gazelle, so the thumb keeps flicking. A bargain site, a job board, a news timeline — all the same chase with different prey.

Rewards of the Self — the private maybes. Mastery, completion, competence: the video-game level, the inbox fought to zero, the skill bar filling. No audience needed — this hunger answers only to itself. It is why a puzzle can hold you at 1 AM with nobody watching, and why "just one more level" is a sentence spoken to no one.
The reward must scratch the itch
Now the chapter's sharpest lesson, taught by a funeral. Two Q&A sites launched in the same era. Mahalo paid cash bounties for good answers. Quora paid nothing — just upvotes and follows.
Pause and guess: Real money versus internet points. Which site thrived, and why?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Quora won, decisively — and Mahalo died. Because the reward must fit the itch. The people who love answering questions are itching for tribal rewards — recognition, respect, the pleasure of being seen as knowledgeable. Cash didn't just miss that itch; it poisoned it — turning a joyful display of expertise into low-paid piecework, and when the bounty stopped clearing the effort, so did the answers. Quora's upvotes fed the actual hunger, at infinite supply and zero cost. The general law: a variable reward is not a prize bolted onto a product — it must answer the specific internal trigger that brought the user in, or the loop never closes. And one more warning rides with it: rewards work only while the user keeps a sense of autonomy. People bristle the instant they feel commanded — psychologists call it reactance — so the products that hold us are the ones that make every visit feel like our own choice. The trap door is always open. That's part of the trap.
The maybes that never run out
One last distinction separates the products that fade from the ones that last decades. Eyal calls it finite versus infinite variability.

A machine with fixed rules — even delicious ones — becomes predictable with enough plays. The mystery depletes like a battery: think of the farm game that conquered the world one year and emptied out the next, its surprises finally all seen. That is finite variability, and it is why most games, shows, and gimmicks have a shelf life.

But some products refresh their own mystery. A feed written by millions of humans. An inbox filled by other people's lives. A platform where the content is other minds — endlessly, unpredictably different. That is infinite variability, and it is the deep reason social products are the stickiest objects ever built: every visitor manufactures surprise for every other visitor. The slot machine that never repeats a spin.
Reading it from both sides of the glass

For the builder: choose the hunger deliberately — tribe, hunt, or self — and make sure the maybe you offer scratches the exact itch your internal trigger identified, preserves the user's sense of free choice, and, if you want to last, renews its own variability instead of depleting it.

For the human: you now hold the diagnostic. Next time an app refuses to let go of you, name the spin — which maybe am I pulling for right now: the tribe's verdict? the hunt's prize? my own scorecard? Naming it doesn't kill the craving. But it moves you one seat back from the lever — from the pigeon to the person watching the pigeon. And notice the tell of every slot machine in your life: you check it without knowing what you're checking for. That's not curiosity. That's the schedule.
Before you go
So the trigger fires, the action completes, the variable reward hits — and the pigeon pecks again. A complete loop... almost.

Because the cleverest step is still missing, and it is the one nobody notices happening. The moment after the reward, when the product asks you for something small — a follow, a photo, a profile detail, a saved playlist. Thirty seconds of your effort. And with it, the trap does something no slot machine ever could: it gets better every time you use it — and harder to leave.
Next time: Investment — how your own labor becomes the lock.
Key takeaways
The brain craves anticipation more than reward — dopamine surges for the maybe, and uncertainty multiplies it.
Predictable rewards bore; variable schedules compel — Skinner's pigeons pecked most when the pellet became a gamble.
Three hungers power the maybes: the Tribe (approval), the Hunt (resources and information), the Self (mastery and completion).
The reward must scratch the itch: Quora's respect beat Mahalo's cash because recognition was the real hunger.
Preserve autonomy — people flee what commands them; the stickiest products feel freely chosen.
Finite variability depletes; infinite variability (other people's minds) is why social feeds never run dry.
Your tell: checking without knowing what you're checking for — that's the slot machine's schedule, not your curiosity.
